---
title: "Understanding Nathan's Hot Dog Contest"
author: Amara McGowan
format:
  closeread-html:
    cr-style:
      narrative-background-color-sidebar: darkgreen
      section-background-color: white
      narrative-text-color-sidebar: white
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
hotdog_winners <- read_csv("HotdogData.csv")
hotdogs_per_min <- hotdog_winners |> mutate(hotdogs_per_minute = Dogs/Time)
```

:::{.cr-section}

Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest is a competitive eating contest where people from around
the world try to eat as many hotdogs as possible in a limited amount of time. The contest
has not always had a set duration, but the competition has been fixed at 10 minutes long since
2008. The top five competitors of the contest are awarded large cash prizes, with the 1st place 
prize in 2025 being $10,000. 10 grand is a lot of money. How much does it really take to win 1st
place in Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest? Does it get harder and harder every year? In other 
words, how many hotdogs do the winners eat and do the winners eat more and more hotdogs every year?


:::{#cr-plot1}
```{r}
ggplot(data = hotdog_winners, aes(x = Year, y = Dogs)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Eaten", title = "Total Hotdogs Eaten by Winners Over Time")
```
:::

The plot to the right shows the total hotdogs eaten by every winner over time. Look at the pattern in the plot. As time goes on, winners do tend to eat more hotdogs, but there is a weird spike around 2000. Let's find out what happened to cause the amount of hotdogs winners ate to increase by so much! @cr-plot1

:::{#cr-table1}
```{r}
hotdog_winners |>
  filter(Year >= 1999, Year <= 2003)
```
:::

Taking a closer look at the competitions from 1999 to 2003 shows us who is responsible for the spike in hotdogs consumed in the early 2000s. @cr-table1

In 2000, eating 25.1 hotdogs was enough to claim first place. In 2001, Takeru Kobayashi ate 50, about double the amount of hotdogs eaten by the winner from the year before. How was Kobayashi able to eat double the amount of hotdogs of the previous winner? 

:::{#cr-photo1}
![The Guardian](https://i.guim.co.uk/img/media/c3fb7fa62b488a153448562ae34d77694a64299d/0_0_2849_2848/master/2849.jpg?width=480&dpr=1&s=none&crop=none)
:::

Kobayashi ate so many more hotdogs because...@cr-photo1


:::{#cr-plot2}
```{r}
ggplot(data = hotdogs_per_min, aes(x = Year, y = hotdogs_per_minute)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(y = "Hotdogs Consumed per min", title = "Amount of Hotdogs Consumed per by Winners")
```
:::

What's wrong with this plot? The general pattern is still similar. Why might that be? 

One explanation is that the duration of the competition changes. When we look at the competetion in its earlier 5 years compared to its latest five years. @cr-table1

:::{#cr-table1}

```{r}
earliest_five <- hotdogs_per_min |>
  arrange(Year) |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

latest_five <- hotdogs_per_min |>
  arrange(desc(Year)) |>
  slice_head(n = 5)

table <- bind_rows(earliest_five, latest_five) |>
  arrange(Year)

table
```
:::

The earliest five winners competed in competitions all under 5 minutes long, while the most recent five winners, some years repeating, are 10 minutes long. This means in earlier years they were eating for less time and therefore may have been able to eat more for longer, whereas the more recent competitors may have slowed down after the first few minutes of eating, lowering their average hotdog per minute rate. 

That still doesn't explain why there is a clear jump around the early 2000s. @cr-plot2

What happened there? Let's find out who won that year! @cr-table2

:::{#cr-table2}
```{r}
hotdogs_per_min |>
  filter(Year > 1998) |>
  arrange((Year)) |>
  slice_head(n = 5)
```

:::

Wow! Look at the jump 2000 to 2001, 21.5 hotdogs to 50 hotdogs total and the hotdogs_per_minute doubled too! Who is Takeru Kobayashi? Take a moment to google him. 

:::
